summary: Every user row must be returned and any name may require correction; an ordered primary-key scan with constant-bounded scalar string work matches this linear output and input lower bound.

optimality.argument: The result contains one row for each of the R users, and changing any input name can change its corresponding output, so exact production requires Omega(R) work. Scanning the primary-key index in order and applying bounded-length scalar string functions once per row takes O(R) work.

replacement_checks.evidence: The query must emit R rows, and every input name independently influences one required output value, establishing an Omega(R) worst-case lower bound.
An ascending primary-key scan supplies the required order while uppercase, lowercase, substring, and concatenation perform constant-bounded work once per row.

Authored fixtures cover the public sample, one-character names, already normalized names, all-uppercase and all-lowercase names, arbitrary internal capitalization, identifier gaps, and input-order independence.
